data.title: Cosmo N.V. GAAP EPS of -€0.28, revenue of €50.24M
data.description:
• Cosmo N.V. press release (CMOPF): 1H GAAP EPS of -€0.28.
  • Revenue of €50.24M (-2.9% Y/Y), while recurring revenue grew 18% YoY to reach €49.58M.
  • Cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments totaled €205.15M.

data.title: First American targets Endpoint scale by end of 2027 while expanding Sequoia toward 70% purchase automation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First American Financial Corporation (FAF) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Our earnings momentum continued in the second quarter as we generated adjusted earnings per share of $2.08, an increase of 36% from the prior year," said CEO Mark Seaton, highlighting "Commercial continued to be a standout performer" with "Revenue increased 34%, setting a second quarter record" and "We closed 14 transactions, generating more than $1 million of premium."

  • Seaton positioned First American Trust as a key earnings lever, saying, "One of the most important earnings drivers continues to be our bank" and noting "average deposits totaled $7.9 billion" with "36% of deposits" from outside captive title operations; he added that ServiceMac "accounted for $1.7 billion of deposits" and that "36%" and the deposit sources "provide meaningful long-term growth opportunities while reinforcing the bank's role as a valuable countercyclical earnings driver."

  • Seaton framed AI execution as the primary strategy, saying, "Our primary strategic priority remains leveraging AI across the enterprise" and citing operational and product examples including "we reduced the time required by 97%" for updating "1,300 forms," and that Exam Assist QC "has now processed more than 50,000 orders, delivering 92% with no additional human review."

  • CFO Matthew Wajner summarized results, saying, "This quarter, we generated GAAP earnings of $2.12 per diluted share" and "adjusted total revenue was $2 billion, up 14%" in the title segment, while commercial revenue was "$314 million" and purchase and refinance trends reflected housing affordability and rate-driven volatility.

Outlook

  • Seaton said, "We remain optimistic about our earnings trajectory for the second half of the year," reiterating, "we continue to believe" commercial is "on pace to deliver a record year" and adding, "Our commercial pipeline has never been stronger" with "commercial open orders" up "9%" over the first "3 weeks" of July.

  • On residential purchase, Seaton stated, "We remain more cautious than the broader consensus" and said, "Through the first 3 weeks of July, our open purchase orders are flat relative to last year as existing home sales remained sluggish."

  • On platform deployment timelines and targets, Seaton said Endpoint is expected to scale "across our local title branch network by the end of 2027" and that Sequoia aims over time to "automate title decisioning for approximately 70% of purchase transactions and 80% of refinanced transactions" in relevant markets, with Sequoia expected "by year-end" to be "deployed across California and Florida" and a "broader national rollout planned for 2027."

Financial Results

  • Wajner reported title segment details including "Commercial revenue was $314 million" and "Average revenue per order was $19,980 per transaction," while purchase revenue increased "2%" and refinance revenue increased "18%"; he also said "Refinance accounted for just 5% of our direct revenue" in the quarter.

  • Wajner said "Investment income was $164 million" and "Interest expense was $30 million," and described cost trends including "Personnel costs were $572 million" and "Other operating expenses were $319 million," while noting the success ratio was "66%" and tied it to "investments" such as ServiceMac.

  • Capital and balance sheet items included Wajner’s statement that the company repurchased "330,000 shares" for "$20 million" at an "average price of $61.99," and that debt-to-capital was "31.4%" ("21.5%" excluding secured financings payable).

Q&A

  • Terry Ma, Barclays: asked about deposit growth sustainability and investment income cadence; CEO Mark Seaton said, "we have a bank, and it's a real strategic advantage for us" and described third-party deposit initiatives, while CFO Wajner said, "investment income net of interest expense grew 8% year-over-year" and added, "I think that 8% is a good proxy for the growth that you'll see in the back half of the year."

  • Terry Ma, Barclays: asked about commercial ARPO durability; CEO Seaton replied, "We think ARPO continue to grow in the second half of the year" and said the company feels it is "still in the early innings of the next commercial real estate cycle."

  • Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens: asked about title margin trajectory and expense efficiency; CFO Wajner said title segment margin year-to-date was "12.3%" and added expansion depends on commercial and is "hard to forecast," while explaining the "66%" success ratio was influenced by ServiceMac investments.

  • Oscar Nieves Santana, Stephens: asked about buyback pace and debt-to-capital; CEO Seaton said repurchases are "opportunistic" and added, "The debt to cap doesn't really play into that now," while noting, "Our target debt to cap is 20%, and we're a little bit higher than that now."

  • Bose George, KBW: asked why purchase ARPO rose and requested commercial asset class mix plus regulatory update; CFO Wajner attributed purchase ARPO to "geographic mix, particularly California," CEO Seaton said industrial was "23%" of premium and that "data center revenue is up 140% -- 147%" year-over-year, and on the FHFA-related post he said, "Haven't heard anything incremental about that."

  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank: asked about which asset classes are growing and data center premium dynamics; CEO Seaton cited development sites up "33%," multifamily up "23%," retail up "59%" and said data centers involve "$1 billion deals" with premiums that can be "$1 million-plus," while adding he was "not really sure" how the three-step premium sizes rank.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical, concentrating on durability of commercial pricing/ARPO, margin sustainability, capital return pacing, and regulatory risk; questions repeatedly pressed on sustainability and forecasting difficulty, including concerns implied by asking "how sustainable" deposit inflows and ARPO increases are and whether there was "anything incremental" on FHFA.

  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident, emphasizing execution and long-run platforms, with Seaton stating, "We remain optimistic" and "Our commercial pipeline has never been stronger," while also using measured language on residential purchase markets and regulatory items such as "wait-and-see mode."

  • Versus the prior quarter, management maintained similar confidence on commercial and AI rollout timelines, while analysts in Q2 leaned more into margin mechanics, bank deposit sustainability, and the FHFA-related headline risk rather than broader AI-disruption framing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Commercial remained the central growth narrative, shifting from Q1’s emphasis on record first-quarter performance and energy/data center tailwinds to Q2’s focus on record second-quarter revenue, larger-deal premium contribution, and broader asset-class strength (including Seaton’s Q2 claim that "10 of our 11 asset classes" grew year-over-year).

  • Residential purchase commentary stayed cautious in both quarters, but the near-term datapoint moved from Q1 open purchase orders down "3%" in early April to Q2 open purchase orders "flat" in the first three weeks of July.

  • The bank and deposits grew in prominence, with Q2 highlighting higher average deposits and a larger share from non-captive sources than Q1, and Q2 Q&A drilling into sustainability and net investment income trends.

  • Platform execution advanced from Q1 pilots and early automation to Q2’s first local title office conversion in Spokane and higher reported automation rates and broader Sequoia county footprint.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macro sensitivity in residential activity, with Seaton noting "affordability challenges" and that refinance volumes "have moderated" as rates moved higher; Wajner also described refinance as "challenged" versus historical levels.

  • Expense and investment tradeoffs were flagged through Wajner’s explanation that the success ratio ran above target due to growth investments, and through the pairing of higher investment income with higher interest expense as deposits expand.

  • Regulatory uncertainty surfaced in Q&A, with Seaton stating, "Haven't heard anything incremental" regarding the FHFA-related commentary, leaving timing and scope unclear.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a continuation of earnings momentum led by commercial pricing and large-transaction activity, while positioning First American Trust deposit growth as a durable, countercyclical contributor to investment income. The second-half outlook leaned on a “never been stronger” commercial pipeline, continued caution on residential purchase demand, and accelerating AI-led operational changes, including scaling Endpoint through 2027 and expanding Sequoia toward long-run automation targets across key states ahead of a planned national rollout in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hims & Hers gains as FDA panel endorses peptide BPC-157
data.description:
Shares of Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) rose more than 10% on Thursday after an FDA advisory panel ​voted to include a popular peptide known as ​BPC-157 in a list of molecules that compounding pharmacies use to make customized treatments.

The FDA’s pharmacy compounding advisory committee voted 8–6 to place BPC-157 free base form on the agency’s 503A Bulks List and voted 8–6 with one abstention to place BPC-157 Acetate form on the same list.

Compounding pharmacies are allowed to use constituents of the 503A Bulks List for the production of customized treatments.

The voting comes on the first day of a two-day meeting scheduled to discuss seven peptide products that the agency removed in 2023 from the list.

The FDA's advisory committees issue non-binding recommendations. However, the regulator usually follows them before making a final decision on authorizations.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), which markets compounding drugs, acquired a peptide facility in California last year. The company is poised to capture a fifth of the telehealth peptide market, expected to reach $2.2B in 2027, according to Leerink Partners.

data.title: Arista Networks sees largely bullish views at BofA ahead of Q2 results
data.description:
BofA maintained Arista Networks' (ANET) Buy rating and $200 price objective ahead of the company's second quarter results on Aug. 4, citing its "expanding AI opportunity, Cloud Titan strength, and growing campus presence." However, the firm also highlighted margin pressure.

Shares of Arista climbed about 1% on Thursday.

"The stock is up 33% YTD versus NASDAQ up 11%, driven by accelerating AI networking demand. We focus on three factors into the print: (1) progress toward the $3.5bn AI fabric revenue target for 2026 (2) deferred revenue trends amid customers’ completion of DC buildouts, and (3) gross margin pressure from Cloud Titan mix and higher component costs," said analysts led by Tal Liani.

Big tech hyperscale data center operators are also called as Cloud Titans by some.

The analysts noted that they have modeled the second quarter revenue growth of 27% year-over-year, with a high bar of investor expectations for the full year 2026, modeled at 35% growth versus management's 28% guidance.

"Gross margin remains a key near-term swing factor as higher Cloud Titan mix and component costs offset strong revenue growth. We model 2Q gross margin of 62.5%, reflecting ~300bps of YoY pressure," said Liani and his team.

data.title: Galderma Group AG Non-GAAP EPS of $2.34, net sales of $3.13B
CrowdStrike extends slide for seventh straight session
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD) extended its recent decline on Thursday, with the stock declining 2.38% at $183.94 in afternoon trading, marking a seventh consecutive session in the red.

Shares have fallen 10.59% over the past six trading sessions, underperforming the S&P 500, which declined 0.60% over the same period. Separately, year to date, CRWD shares are up 58.85%, compared to a 9.55% gain for the S&P 500.

Despite the recent weakness in the stock, CRWD continues to expand its AI capabilities. The company  recently partnered with AI chipmaker Cerebras Systems to integrate Cerebras' inference technology into its Falcon AI Detection and Response platform, a move aimed at accelerating AI-powered threat detection while strengthening security across Cerebras' operations.

Seeking Alpha analyst Bay Area Ideas maintained a Hold rating on CrowdStrike, stating the company's leadership position in cybersecurity, expanding market opportunity, strong adoption, and solid financial performance. He added FY2027 guidance points to accelerating revenue and annual recurring revenue growth, with operating income and EPS expected to increase year over year. However, the analyst believes the stock's elevated valuation limits further upside potential, supporting the continued Hold rating.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings system assigned CRWD a Hold rating with a score of 3.48 out of 5. The stock receives an A grade for profitability and a B grade for growth, while valuation remains a key concern with an F grade.

Wall Street analysts remain largely positive, with 40 analysts assigning a Buy rating or higher, while 11 analysts maintain a Hold rating and one analyst rates the stock a Sell or lower. Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analysts maintain a more cautious view, with a Hold consensus rating on the shares.

data.title: Getty Realty anticipates $2.52-$2.54 2026 AFFO per share as it expands investment activity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Getty Realty Corp. (GTY) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Our results for the second quarter reflect both of these dynamics as we increased our annualized base rent by 15%, grew our AFFO per share by 5.1% and increased our full year 2026 earnings guidance for the second time this year.” (President, CEO & Director Christopher Constant)
  • “Year-to-date, we have deployed more than $172 million at an initial cash yield of 7.6%.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • “At quarter end, our lease portfolio included 1,220 net lease properties and 1 active redevelopment site... occupancy was 99.8% and our weighted average lease term was 10.3 years.” (Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Robert Ryan)
  • “AFFO per share was $0.62 in Q2 2026... As a result of our year-to-date investment activity, we are increasing our full year 2026 AFFO per share guidance to a range of $2.52 to $2.54 from our prior guidance of $2.50 to $2.52.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Brian Dickman)

Outlook

  • “We are increasing our full year 2026 AFFO per share guidance to a range of $2.52 to $2.54 from our prior guidance of $2.50 to $2.52.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “Our guidance reflects the current run rate from our in-place portfolio with certain expense and credit loss variability and does not include any prospective investment or capital activities.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing, management again emphasized that guidance excludes future deal activity, while pointing to higher year-to-date deployment and a larger closed investment quarter; in Q1, the company had raised guidance to “$2.50 to $2.52 from the prior range of $2.48 to $2.50.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Financial Results

  • “AFFO per share was $0.62 in Q2 2026 and $1.25 for the first half of 2026.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “That ratio was 9.3% for Q2 2026... we expect full-year G&A growth to be less than 2% and for our G&A ratio to fall below 9% as we continue to benefit from our efforts to scale the company while maintaining appropriate levels of overhead.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “We ended the quarter with approximately $1.1 billion of total debt outstanding... We have no debt maturities until June 2028.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • “We currently have 5.8 million shares of common stock subject to outstanding forward sale agreements, which upon settlement are anticipated to raise gross proceeds of approximately $190.5 million.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Q&A

  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: Asked what is driving QSR momentum and whether the team has expanded; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “it’s the success of the person we brought on... it takes time to build relationships... we anticipate balanced volumes across the investment program.”
  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: Asked why Q2 cash yields were at the lower end versus “mid- to high 7%”; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “there’s a lot of volume in that kind of mid-7% range,” and Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman added, “the improving cost of capital... opening up opportunities for us to compete... our spreads have largely remained constant.”
  • Daniel Byun (for Jana Galan), Bank of America: Asked whether “$19.3 million advanced aggregate funding” is included in the $95 million under contract; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “No, that would have already been deployed.”
  • Daniel Byun (for Jana Galan), Bank of America: Asked about rent coverage and a higher sub-1x bucket; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “No, certainly no softening... same portfolio of ramping new-to-industry car washes.”
  • Upal Rana, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Asked whether improved cost of capital expanded the investment pool; Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said, “it’s just opening up more opportunities,” and later President, CEO & Director Constant said, “what we’ve been messaging is what we’ve done over the last couple of years, we view as the floor.”
  • Robert Stevenson, Huntington: Asked about new asset types and scaling development funding; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “we’re trying to be really thoughtful as we think about extending beyond the 4 asset classes,” and on development funding, “we view it as another path to fee ownership and another path to growth.”
  • Robert Stevenson, Huntington: Asked whether Q2 dispositions were defensive; management responded that sales were selective, saying, “it was just $8 million, handful of properties... a mix.” (Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Robert Ryan)
  • Robert Stevenson, Huntington: Asked where a new 10-year note would price; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “a 10-year note for us... would be about 6.25%.”
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Asked how to think about acquisition visibility versus reported pipeline; Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said, “there’s always things that close that never hit the pipeline.”
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Asked about “the right level of forward liquidity”; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “the best word is balance,” adding the company does not want to be “too long, too much equity at a lower price.”
  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked how fuel price moves could affect coverage; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “margins continue to hold,” and “we’re not expecting to see any massive fluctuation.”
  • Michael Gorman, BTIG: Asked whether strong C-store margins are driving M&A and about geopolitical impacts; President, CEO & Director Constant said, “I don’t think today’s margin environment is really what’s driving increased M&A,” while adding operators’ “core businesses remain healthy.”
  • Michael Gorman, BTIG: Asked about credit losses and assumptions; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said, “No realized credit losses to date,” and “we continue to use a 25 basis point assumption.”
  • Wesley Golladay, Baird: Asked whether faster underwriting is due to volume or systems; Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said, “candidly, I think it’s probably both.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive but probing on pricing, underwriting pace, tenant health buckets, funding strategy, and credit losses, including requests to “clarify” pipeline items and questions around cap rates, visibility, and coverage mix. (Multiple analysts)
  • Management tone was slightly positive and operationally confident in prepared remarks and generally steady in Q&A, repeatedly pointing to resilience and process advantages, including, “we are confident that we can deploy this capital in a productive and accretive manner.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • Versus the prior quarter, management used similar confidence language on portfolio durability and transaction markets, while Q2 added more detailed pushback on credit/coverage concerns, including, “No, certainly no softening.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management raised 2026 AFFO per share guidance again in Q2, moving from Q1’s “$2.50 to $2.52” to “$2.52 to $2.54,” and maintained that guidance excludes future investments and capital markets actions. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • The investment narrative shifted from Q1’s smaller quarter and larger under-contract figure to Q2’s materially higher closed activity and a smaller under-contract figure, with Q2 emphasizing increased velocity and funding capacity, including “more than $190 million of unsettled forward equity.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • Analysts’ focus stayed consistent quarter-to-quarter around pipeline momentum, cap-rate/yield bands, rent coverage buckets, and capital strategy; Q2 also added explicit questions on forward equity philosophy and debt pricing. (Multiple analysts)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged macro uncertainty, noting “economic volatility driven by geopolitical events,” while stating portfolio performance has remained resilient. (President, CEO & Director Constant)
  • Credit and tenant health scrutiny centered on weaker rent-coverage buckets; management attributed this to “ramping new-to-industry car washes” and said, “nothing that’s causing us any great concern at this point.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)
  • Capital markets sensitivity was discussed through the lens of rate levels and equity timing, with management cautioning against being “too long, too much equity at a lower price.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as a combination of steady in-place cash flows and accelerating external growth, highlighting higher year-to-date investment deployment, a largely full portfolio with long lease duration, and a second increase to full-year 2026 AFFO per share guidance. In Q&A, executives emphasized improving cost of capital, disciplined pricing in the mid-7% yield range, and stable tenant fundamentals, while addressing analyst scrutiny on rent-coverage buckets, forward equity strategy, and refinancing costs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Airlines anticipates full-year adjusted EPS to be break-even at midpoint amid $3.75/gal Q3 fuel forecast
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Airlines Group (AAL) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "American delivered a quarter that shows the commercial initiatives we've implemented over the past few years are producing meaningful results," said Robert Isom (CEO, President & Director), adding, "We achieved record quarterly revenue on year-over-year growth of more than 16%."

  • Isom said fuel remained the central headwind: "Executing solidly on that strategy in the second quarter helped offset nearly 50% of the $2.2 billion year-over-year increase in fuel expense," while adding, "We ended the second quarter with over $11 billion in available liquidity."

  • Nathaniel Pieper (Chief Commercial Officer) said revenue strength was broad-based: "Total revenue increasing 16.3% year-over-year," and added that "Domestic unit revenue increased nearly 11% year-over-year," with "Pacific unit revenue increased 15% year-over-year" and "Latin America unit revenue was up approximately 7% year-over-year."

  • Pieper highlighted operational/network actions tied to revenue quality, saying, "In April, we made significant changes to the bank structure at our largest hub, Dallas-Fort Worth," and "Misconnects across the system are down nearly 25% year-over-year."

  • Devon May (Executive VP & CFO) framed the quarter around fuel volatility and guidance resets: "In the second quarter, fuel expense increased by over $2.2 billion or 83% year-over-year," and "Since the beginning of July, expected third quarter fuel expense has increased by more than $700 million for the quarter."

Outlook

  • May said fuel assumptions drove capacity and earnings ranges: "As of July 21, we expect an average fuel price of approximately $3.75 per gallon in the third quarter," and "Our third quarter capacity is now expected to be up 3% to 5% year-over-year."

  • May guided to lower near-term profitability: "For the third quarter, we are guiding to an adjusted loss per diluted share of $0.70 to $0.10," and "we are adjusting our full year guidance to between a loss of $0.65 to a profit of $0.65 per diluted share."

  • Isom tied the full-year message directly to fuel: "Even against an expected nearly $6 billion year-over-year fuel headwind, we anticipate full year adjusted earnings to be break-even at the midpoint of our guidance range." Compared with Q1, management shifted from "we expect to be profitable in 2026" (Robert Isom, Q1) to break-even at the midpoint.

Financial Results

  • Isom emphasized operating cost control alongside revenue: "We remain focused on efficiency in the second quarter and held nonfuel year-over-year unit cost growth to under 3%," and he said the quarter ended with "over $11 billion in available liquidity."

  • May provided liquidity and balance-sheet direction: "We ended the quarter with $11.3 billion of liquidity," and "At the midpoint of our current 2026 guide, we expect to produce positive free cash flow for the year and to finish the year with lower net debt than at the start of the year."

  • On investment levels, May reiterated: "We continue to expect approximately $4 billion of capital expenditures" and "We expect to take 48 new aircraft this year."

Q&A

  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: capacity urgency vs. fuel volatility? Robert Isom: "We're matching our network, our capacity to the demand environment," adding, "We're taking a diligent look at the fourth quarter right now."

  • Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: CapEx shape? Devon May: "We'll be right around $4 billion" in 2026 and "for next year, it's probably going to be somewhere around that $4.5 billion range."

  • Andrew Didora, BofA: 2027 labor/CASM? May: "The pilot agreement... becomes amendable in August of 2027" and "you should expect CASMx to grow at around low single digits" with mid-single-digit growth.

  • Didora, BofA: transatlantic trajectory? Pieper: "Our unit revenue in London was up 20% in the quarter" and said Atlantic performance into Q3 was seeing "good results."

  • John Godyn, Citi: premium strategy and loyalty? Pieper: "Our premium revenue in the quarter was up 19%," and "our enrollments are up 32% in the quarter year-over-year."

  • David Vernon, Bernstein: should AA trim network? Pieper: "You can't fight in every battle" and said the airline is "selectively cutting" and cited "trim... and reallocated" in Charlotte.

  • Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: labor-cost gap thesis and premium capacity risk? Isom: "Let's just be clear" and said the focus is "generating more revenue" while adding peers "haven't put" contracts "in place"; Pieper said, "Strong demand for premium products exist everywhere" and "I feel really comfortable with where we are."

  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley: what excites 2027+ and fuel pass-through? Isom: "We anticipate producing free cash flow" despite fuel being "up by over $6 billion," and said profitability is achievable "at any fuel pricing" with capacity and product value aligned.

  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: liquidity/leverage targets and merchandising? May: "We did a little over $1 billion, around $1.3 billion of incremental financings in Q2" and reiterated targets including "inside $35 billion of total debt" and leverage "inside of 3 turns"; Pieper said upsell gains came from evaluating "product offering" and noted an "instant upgrade program."

  • Alison Sider, WSJ: Southwest premium push impact? Pieper: "We're taking share. We're not losing it." Leslie Josephs, CNBC: fuel sourcing and actions? May: "We're around 65% supplied from the Gulf" and said the bigger lever is "how much capacity you want to produce."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, focused on structural profitability and capacity discipline, with questions like "why isn't the low-margin producer cutting capacity?" (Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI) and concerns about a "huge gap in financial performance" (David Vernon, Bernstein).

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks but more defensive in Q&A, using clarifiers such as "Let's just be clear" (Robert Isom, CEO, President & Director) and repeatedly anchoring answers on fuel volatility and revenue initiatives.

  • Versus Q1, management’s tone shifted from confidence in profitability ("we expect to be profitable in 2026," Robert Isom, Q1) to emphasizing near-term uncertainty ("The current fuel curve has dampened our near-term expectations," Devon May, CFO).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 introduced a sharper fuel-driven reset: May said, "Just 3 weeks ago, we were expecting to guide to full year pretax earnings approaching $1.5 billion," but updated to a full-year EPS range of (-$0.65) to $0.65.

  • Commercial momentum language strengthened, with Isom in Q2 stating, "We achieved record quarterly revenue," while Q1 stressed disruptions ("$320 million revenue impact from winter storms," Robert Isom, Q1).

  • Analysts’ focus broadened from Q1 pricing discipline and partnerships (including Alaska discussion) toward Q2 questions centered on capacity cuts, margin gap persistence, and liquidity under fuel volatility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Fuel price volatility dominated: May said expected Q3 fuel expense had increased "more than $700 million" since early July and cited "volatile fuel environment."

  • Capacity reductions risk unit costs: May said "CASMx in the quarter is slightly elevated due to the capacity reductions" and management will "continue to evaluate capacity levels based on both fuel prices and demand trends."

  • Competitive/structural questions persisted: analysts raised whether the network should be smaller and whether premium capacity could be "too much" (Jamie Baker, JPMorgan), while Pieper said the airline is "being very targeted" and "selectively cutting."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a record-revenue quarter driven by the four-pillar commercial plan, but repeatedly framed the investment case around fuel volatility resetting near-term earnings expectations; leadership maintained that revenue momentum, cost discipline, and planned premium/network initiatives can support positive free cash flow and balance-sheet improvement even as guidance ranges widened and capacity plans were adjusted in response to higher expected fuel prices.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
